recipes.title: Lebanese Baklava
ingredients: For syrup: 2 cups sugar, 1 cup water, 1 tbsp lemon juice, 2 tbsp orange blossom water
recipes.tips: Cold syrup on hot baklava (or vice versa) is the secret to crispy layers. Cut before baking so pieces separate cleanly!
recipes.story: Making baklava was reserved for special occasions. My mother would spend hours layering each sheet with love and precision. The smell of butter and nuts baking is the smell of celebration.
recipes.description: Layers of crispy phyllo filled with spiced nuts, drenched in fragrant orange blossom syrup.
instructions: Make syrup first: boil sugar and water, add lemon juice, simmer 10 minutes, add orange blossom water. Cool completely.
Process nuts with sugar and spices until coarsely ground.
Brush baking pan with butter. Layer 8 phyllo sheets, brushing each with butter.
Spread thin layer of nut mixture. Add 2 sheets, brush with butter.
Repeat layers until nuts are used. Top with 8 sheets.
Cut into diamond shapes before baking.
Bake at 350°F for 40-45 minutes until golden.
Pour cold syrup over hot baklava immediately.
Let cool completely before serving.

recipes.tips: Add the pita chips right before serving to keep them crispy. Sumac is essential - it gives fattoush its signature tangy flavor!
recipes.story: Fattoush was born from thrift - using leftover stale bread. Now it's a beloved summer staple, best enjoyed with family under the grapevine shade.
instructions: Toast or fry pita until crispy, break into pieces.
Combine all vegetables and herbs in a large bowl.
Whisk together olive oil, lemon juice, sumac, garlic, and salt.
Just before serving, add pita chips and dressing.
Toss gently and garnish with pomegranate seeds.
Serve immediately while pita is crispy.
recipes.description: Vibrant Lebanese salad with crispy pita chips, fresh vegetables, and tangy sumac dressing.
recipes.title: Fattoush Salad

recipes.description: Creamy, silky smooth hummus made with perfectly cooked chickpeas, tahini, lemon, and garlicc.
instructions: Add tahini and lemon juice to a food processor. Process for 1-2 minutes until light and fluffy.
Add garlic, cumin, and salt. Process for 30 seconds.
Add half of the chickpeas and process for 1 minute.
Add remaining chickpeas and process while slowly drizzling in the reserved liquid.
Continue processing until completely smooth, about 2-3 minutes.
Taste and adjust seasonings as needed.
Transfer to a serving bowl, create a swirl, drizzle with olive oil.
Garnish with paprika and fresh parsley.
ingredients: Extra virgin olive oil for serving
Paprika and fresh parsley for garnish
recipes.tips: The secret to restaurant-quality hummus is processing the tahini and lemon first, and using warm chickpeas. Removing the chickpea skins makes it even silkier!
recipes.story: Hummus has been the heart of our family gatherings for generations. My grandmother would wake up early to soak the chickpeas overnight, cooking them slowly until they melted in your mouth. This recipe carries her legacy.
recipes.title: Classic Hummus

instructions: Soak bulgur in cold water for 30 minutes, drain and squeeze dry.
Process shell meat and onion until paste-like. Mix with bulgur and spices.
For filling, sauté onion, add lamb, pine nuts, and spices. Cool.
Wet hands, take a ball of shell mixture, form into oval.
Make a cavity, add filling, seal and shape into torpedo.
Deep fry at 350°F until deep golden brown.
Drain on paper towels and serve hot with yogurt.
recipes.description: Crispy torpedo-shaped shells of bulgur and meat, filled with spiced lamb and pine nuts.
recipes.tips: Keep your hands wet when shaping kibbeh. The shell should be thin and even. My mother always said the walls should be "thin as a wish"!
recipes.story: Making kibbeh is a family affair. We would gather around the table, each person with a task - someone mixing, someone shaping, someone frying. The stories shared were as important as the food itself.
ingredients: For shell: 1 cup fine bulgur, 1 lb lean ground beef, 1 onion, salt, allspice
For filling: 1/2 lb ground lamb, 1/2 cup pine nuts, 1 onion diced, allspice, cinnamon
recipes.title: Kibbeh
Palestinian Knafeh
instructions: Make syrup: Combine sugar, water, and lemon juice. Boil for 10 minutes, add rose water, cool.
Process kataifi until finely shredded. Mix with melted butter and food coloring.
Press half the kataifi mixture into a buttered round pan.
Slice the soaked cheese and layer evenly over the kataifi.
Top with remaining kataifi mixture, pressing firmly.
Bake at 350°F for 45 minutes until golden.
Flip onto serving platter (cheese side up).
Pour cold syrup over hot knafeh.
Garnish generously with crushed pistachios.
Serve immediately while cheese is stretchy.
recipes.description: A beloved Palestinian dessert of crispy shredded phyllo with stretchy cheese, soaked in sweet rose syrup.
recipes.story: Nablus is famous for knafeh, and every family has their own secret touch. Ours uses a mix of cheeses passed down from my great-grandmother in Nablus. It's tradition to serve it at every celebration.
recipes.tips: The secret to perfect knafeh is using well-desalinated cheese and serving it immediately. The contrast of hot, crispy pastry with cold syrup is essential!
ingredients: 1 lb akkawi cheese (or mozzarella), soaked overnight

recipes.description: Jordanian-Palestinian celebratory dish of lamb cooked in fermented dried yogurt, served over rice.
instructions: Soak jameed in warm water until soft, then blend until smooth.
Boil lamb with onion, cardamom, and bay leaves until tender, about 1.5 hours.
Add reconstituted jameed to the pot, simmer gently (do not boil hard).
Cook rice separately with turmeric.
Layer on a large platter: bread, rice, lamb, then pour jameed sauce over.
Garnish with toasted nuts and fresh parsley.
Traditionally eaten with right hand, forming balls of rice.
recipes.story: Mansaf is the dish of hospitality and celebration. It's served at weddings, graduations, and homecomings. Sharing mansaf from a communal platter is a profound act of togetherness.
recipes.tips: Never let the jameed sauce boil vigorously once added, or it will curdle. The sauce should be poured generously - mansaf should be "swimming"!
ingredients: 1/2 cup pine nuts and almonds, toasted
recipes.title: Mansaf

recipes.tips: The key to a perfect maqluba is not stirring once layered, and letting it rest before flipping. This allows everything to set properly for that dramatic reveal!
recipes.story: Maqluba means "upside down" in Arabic. Every family gathering in Palestine features this magnificent dish. The moment of flipping the pot is always met with anticipation and cheers.
recipes.description: A stunning Palestinian dish of layered rice, vegetables, and tender meat, flipped upside down for a dramatic presentation.
instructions: Fry eggplant and potato slices until golden. Fry cauliflower florets until golden. Set aside.
In a large pot, brown the chicken or lamb with onion and spices.
Add water to cover and simmer until meat is almost cooked, about 30 minutes.
In a heavy-bottomed pot, layer: fried vegetables, then meat, then rice.
Pour hot broth over everything, ensuring rice is just covered.
Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to lowest setting.
Cover tightly and cook for 25-30 minutes until rice is done.
Let rest for 10 minutes, then flip onto a large serving platter.
Garnish with toasted nuts and fresh parsley.
recipes.title: Maqluba (Upside-Down Rice)

recipes.story: Musakhan is the national dish of Palestine. It was traditionally made during olive harvest season, celebrating the new oil. Every bite is a taste of Palestinian heritage.
recipes.tips: The onions must be cooked low and slow until they're deeply caramelized - this is what makes musakhan special. Use the best olive oil you can find!
instructions: Season chicken with salt, pepper, and half the sumac. Roast until cooked.
Slowly caramelize onions in olive oil until deeply golden, about 45 minutes.
Add remaining sumac and allspice to onions.
Warm flatbreads, then spread generously with onion mixture.
Place roasted chicken pieces on top.
Drizzle with more olive oil and top with toasted pine nuts.
Traditionally eaten with hands, rolling up the bread with chicken.
recipes.description: Iconic Palestinian dish of roasted chicken on sumac-onion flatbread with toasted pine nuts.
recipes.title: Musakhan
Chicken Shawarma
recipes.tips: Marinate overnight for the best flavor. Cook on high heat to get that signature char while keeping the inside juicy!
recipes.story: Street shawarma in Beirut is an experience like no other. The smell of spiced meat spinning on vertical rotisseries fills the streets at night. This recipe brings that magic home.
ingredients: Pita bread, pickles, tomatoes, toum (garlic sauce)
instructions: Mix all spices with olive oil, garlic, and lemon juice.
Marinate chicken for at least 2 hours, preferably overnight.
Cook chicken on high heat until charred and cooked through.
Let rest, then slice thinly against the grain.
Warm pita bread.
Fill with chicken, toum, pickles, and vegetables.
Roll tightly and enjoy!
recipes.description: Tender, aromatic spiced chicken in warm pita with garlic sauce and fresh vegetables.

ingredients: Salt and black pepper to taste
recipes.story: In our village, we would pick fresh parsley from our garden every Friday morning to make tabbouleh for the weekend gathering. The scent of freshly chopped herbs still takes me home.
recipes.tips: Authentic Lebanese tabbouleh is mostly parsley with just a little bulgur. Make sure your parsley is completely dry before chopping, and use the best olive oil you can find!
instructions: Soak bulgur in cold water for 30 minutes, then drain and squeeze dry.
Wash and thoroughly dry parsley. Chop very finely.
Combine parsley, mint, tomatoes, and green onions in a large bowl.
Add the prepared bulgur and mix gently.
Whisk together olive oil, lemon juice, salt, and pepper.
Pour dressing over salad and toss to combine.
Let sit for 15 minutes before serving.
Serve with romaine lettuce leaves for scooping.
recipes.description: Fresh parsley salad with bulgur, tomatoes, mint, and a bright lemon-olive oil dressing.
recipes.title: Traditional Tabbouleh

news.title: New Cookbook: "From Our Kitchen to Yours" Coming Soon
news.content:
After years of sharing recipes on this website, we're thrilled to announce that our family cookbook is finally becoming a reality.

## What to Expect

**100 Family Recipes** - From everyday meals to celebration feasts, these are the dishes that have defined our family gatherings for generations.

**Stories Behind Every Dish** - Each recipe comes with the story of who made it, when we ate it, and why it matters.

**Technique Guides** - Learn to make perfect kibbeh shells, stretch phyllo thin, and roll grape leaves like a pro.

**Beautiful Photography** - Full-color images of finished dishes and step-by-step photos for complex techniques.

## Preview Chapters

1. **Morning Rituals** - Breakfast traditions
2. **The Mezze Table** - Appetizers and dips
3. **From the Sea** - Seafood dishes
4. **Celebration Feasts** - Main courses for special occasions
5. **Sweet Endings** - Desserts and sweets
6. **Mama Montaha's Secrets** - Tips and techniques

## Pre-Order Information

The cookbook will be available for pre-order starting next month. Sign up for our newsletter to be notified when it launches.

This book is our love letter to Palestinian and Lebanese cuisine. We can't wait to share it with you.
news.excerpt: We're excited to announce our upcoming cookbook featuring 100 family recipes.

news.title: Spring Cooking Classes Now Open for Registration
news.excerpt: Learn to make traditional dishes in our hands-on cooking classes this spring.
news.content:
Join us for our spring session of cooking classes! Whether you're a beginner or experienced cook, you'll learn authentic techniques passed down through generations.

## Available Classes

### Mezze Mastery (March 20)
Learn to make hummus, baba ganoush, labneh, and fattoush. Perfect for hosting gatherings.
- Duration: 3 hours
- Skill level: Beginner
- Price: $85

### The Art of Kibbeh (April 3)
Master the technique of forming perfect kibbeh. Includes three varieties: fried, baked, and raw.
- Duration: 4 hours
- Skill level: Intermediate
- Price: $95

### Palestinian Feast (April 17)
Prepare a full meal: musakhan, maqluba, and knafeh. A comprehensive class for serious cooks.
- Duration: 5 hours
- Skill level: Intermediate
- Price: $120

### Bread Workshop (May 1)
Hands-on bread making: pita, man'oushe, and saj bread. Take home your creations.
- Duration: 4 hours
- Skill level: All levels
- Price: $90

## What's Included

- All ingredients and equipment
- Printed recipes to take home
- Tastings of everything we make
- A small gift from our kitchen

## Location

Classes are held at our teaching kitchen in Los Angeles. Limited to 10 participants for personalized attention.

Registration opens March 1st. Spots fill quickly, so register early!

news.title: Eid Traditions: The Foods That Define Our Celebrations
news.content:
Eid is a time of joy, family gatherings, and most importantly—sweets. The tradition of preparing special treats for Eid goes back generations.

## Essential Eid Sweets

### Ma'amoul
These stuffed cookies filled with dates, walnuts, or pistachios are perhaps the most iconic Eid treat. Families spend days before Eid making hundreds of pieces.

### Ka'ak (Eid Cookies)
Ring-shaped cookies decorated with intricate patterns using special molds. Each family has their own recipe and design.

### Baklava
Layers of phyllo, nuts, and syrup. No Eid table is complete without a tray of baklava.

### Halawet el-Jibn
Sweet cheese rolls filled with clotted cream, drizzled with syrup and topped with pistachios.

## The Making Process

In traditional homes, Eid preparation begins a week before. Women gather to make sweets together, sharing stories and laughter. Children help by pressing patterns into cookies or filling ma'amoul.

## Modern Adaptations

While traditions remain strong, some families now:
- Reduce sugar in recipes for health
- Create vegan versions of classic sweets
- Experiment with new flavors while keeping traditional shapes

However you celebrate, may your Eid be sweet, your table full, and your heart at peace.

Eid Mubarak!
news.excerpt: From ka'ak to ma'amoul, explore the sweet treats that make Eid special.

news.title: Olive Harvest Season: Celebrating the Green Gold
news.excerpt: It's olive harvest time in Palestine and Lebanon - a time of family, tradition, and fresh oil.
news.content:
October marks the beginning of olive harvest season across the Levant. For families who have tended olive groves for generations, this is the most important time of year.

## The Harvest Process

### Picking
Olives are traditionally picked by hand or with small rakes. Families gather under trees, spreading tarps to catch falling fruit. It's hard work, but filled with song and stories.

### Pressing
Within days of harvest, olives must be pressed. Modern cold-press facilities have replaced many traditional stone mills, but the anticipation of tasting new oil remains unchanged.

### The First Taste
Dipping fresh bread into newly pressed oil is a moment of celebration. The oil is bright green, peppery, and intensely flavorful.

## Celebrating with Food

Harvest season meals feature olive oil prominently:
- **Zeit w za'atar** - Fresh oil with za'atar for breakfast
- **Musakhan** - The olive oil-drenched Palestinian dish
- **Ful mudammas** - Fava beans swimming in new oil

## How to Participate

Even if you can't visit during harvest, you can:
- Order fresh harvest oil from Palestinian cooperatives
- Make traditional harvest-season recipes
- Share the stories and importance of olive culture

The olive tree is a symbol of resilience and peace. Every drop of oil carries thousands of years of history.

news.title: Preparing for Ramadan 2026: Traditional Recipes to Try
news.content:
As Ramadan 2026 approaches, families around the world are planning their iftar menus. Here are some traditional recipes to make this holy month special.

## Must-Have Dishes

### Soups
Start your iftar with a warming soup. Lentil soup with lemon is classic, or try Palestinian freekeh soup for something heartier.

### Dates and Beverages
Break your fast with dates as per tradition. Prepare qamar al-din (apricot drink) or jallab for refreshing accompaniment.

### Main Courses
Maqluba and stuffed vegetables are perfect for family gatherings. Prepare ingredients in advance to make cooking easier.

### Sweets
Qatayef (stuffed pancakes) are only made during Ramadan. Learn the technique now so you're ready when the month begins.

## Planning Tips

1. **Prep in advance** - Make and freeze kibbeh, falafel, and grape leaves
2. **Stock your pantry** - Ensure you have all spices, tahini, and dried goods
3. **Plan your menu weekly** - This reduces stress and waste
4. **Involve the family** - Cooking together is part of the Ramadan spirit

May this Ramadan bring you peace, blessings, and delicious food shared with loved ones.
news.excerpt: Get ready for the holy month with these time-honored recipes that make iftar special.
